标题: Elon Musk’s Legal War With OpenAI’s Sam Altman Set for Showdown in Court
内容:
The artificial intelligence era’s biggest legal battle heads to a showdown next week, as Elon Musk and
OpenAI
CEO Sam Altman are scheduled to face off in a federal courtroom in Oakland, Calif., over the future of the company that pioneered the AI boom.
The trial in Musk’s lawsuit against Altman, OpenAI and its partner Microsoft starts with jury selection on Monday and is expected to run through mid-May, barring some last-ditch settlement.
People close to both Musk and OpenAI executives have been trying to convince the parties to settle by agreeing to give Musk a piece of the OpenAI foundation, according to several people familiar with the conversations. But it is unclear if either side has been receptive to their suggestions.
The trial promises to be one of the biggest spectacles of power and pettiness that Silicon Valley has produced, with a witness list that includes not only Musk and Altman, but Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella and other current and former top executives from Microsoft, OpenAI and Musk’s corporate empire.
The stakes could be monumental for OpenAI, which Musk co-founded a decade ago with Altman as a nonprofit. Musk alleges the startup broke promises to him and violated its charitable mission in several ways. He is seeking the disgorgement of assets that can be traced back to his original contributions. An economist that Musk hired estimated in December that his interest was
worth up to $109 billion
, at a time when OpenAI was valued at $500 billion. At OpenAI’s latest valuation, that estimated interest could equate to as much as $187 billion.
Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers, the judge hearing the case,
has said
that she didn’t find the methodology of Musk’s economist convincing, saying he appeared to be “pulling these numbers out of the air.”
OpenAI has said that Musk’s claims are a baseless attempt to disrupt its growth by a tycoon who competes with it through xAI, which he founded in 2023. Musk’s AI startup is now part of SpaceX, the conglomerate he leads that is planning a massive IPO for June. An OpenAI spokesperson on Friday said that Musk’s case has been about trying to attack the OpenAI nonprofit. Asked about the possibility of any legal settlement, they said: “We will continue to stand and fight for the mission.”
The implications extend to a host of other tech giants and prominent investors that have pumped money into OpenAI, including Amazon and Nvidia. They are also benefiting enormously from the AI frenzy that OpenAI initiated when it launched ChatGPT more than three years ago. OpenAI this month finalized a $122 billion funding round—the largest in history—which valued it at $852 billion including the new cash.
If a jury finds OpenAI liable, Musk is also asking the judge to issue an order reversing OpenAI’s October restructuring, which converted its for-profit wing to a corporation with traditional equity. OpenAI has said that the restructuring, already approved by regulators, was a necessary step toward its ambition of going public, so unwinding it could obstruct that plan. Lawyers following the case
have said
the court is unlikely to issue such a disruptive order.
Musk also wants the court to order OpenAI to observe its charitable mission by prioritizing work on AI safety and releasing its research publicly, and to remove Altman as a director of OpenAI’s nonprofit board.
“This is going to be an enormously significant case,” said Anupam Chander, who teaches AI regulation and contracts as a law professor at Georgetown University. “This is going to be a trial in the courtroom, but also in the press, where the parties are going to throw stones at each other, but they all live in kind of glass houses.”
“Many Americans don’t see a good guy in this rivalry,” he said. “For many Americans, it’s a pox on both their houses.”
The trial will retrace how Musk donated $38 million to help establish OpenAI in its early days, when it was a charity chartered to ensure that powerful AI benefits humanity. And it will show how Musk had a falling out with his co-founders—including OpenAI President Greg Brockman, whom Musk also names as a defendant—and left the fledgling organization before it took investments from Microsoft and established the for-profit arm to finance its need for pricey AI chips.
Musk alleges that by taking Microsoft’s money, keeping OpenAI’s technology proprietary and deprioritizing safety efforts, OpenAI committed fraud, was unjustly enriched and breached its “charitable trust.” He claims Microsoft abetted that breach, in part by interfering in the OpenAI board’s brief ouster of Altman in 2023.
Musk first sued OpenAI two years ago, then
dropped that suit and refiled
after hiring Marc Toberoff, a prominent Hollywood attorney with a flair for drama. In the renewed complaint, Toberoff set the tone for the lawsuit, writing that OpenAI’s “perfidy and deceit are of Shakespearean proportions.”
Since Musk filed the suit, he and Altman have traded insults and accusations in an endless series of social media posts and legal filings—one reason people have been skeptical that a settlement could be reached. Altman has accused xAI of creating “anime sex bots for children,” while Musk has referred to him on X as “Scam Altman.”
Among the unresolved legal questions on the trial’s eve: whether OpenAI’s nonprofit or for-profit entity would have to disgorge billions of dollars if it loses, and who would receive those funds.
Musk has said he doesn’t want any money for himself, but wants the OpenAI corporation to transfer any money to its nonprofit. Microsoft countered
in a legal filing
Wednesday that such an outcome is “anomalous and untenable” because it would return money to the very nonprofit that Musk alleges betrayed its mission.
Musk’s lawyers have said he would drop his fraud claims as long as the court agrees to proceed on the claims of breach of charitable trust and unjust enrichment.
OpenAI’s lawyers responded
in a legal filing
Wednesday that Musk shouldn’t be allowed to make his decision conditional on the court. They seized on his latest move to argue that Musk “has no standing to sue on behalf of the public [or] the OpenAI nonprofit.” If Musk no longer says he was injured personally, their argument goes, that leaves him without standing to argue for OpenAI to give up its money. “A plaintiff cannot come to court just to air a grievance,” they said.
Late Friday, Judge Gonzalez Rogers affirmed that the breach of charitable trust and unjust enrichment claims are going to trial, and therefore dismissed the fraud claims as Musk’s lawyers requested.
During evidence discovery, both parties unearthed email records supporting their sides. Musk’s lawyers will invoke threads in which Altman stated he was enthusiastic about OpenAI maintaining its charitable structure and
private notes from Brockman
contemplating the fastest way he could become a billionaire.
OpenAI’s lawyers are sure to point to email records of Musk proposing that OpenAI could merge with Tesla, his own for-profit corporation. They will also argue that Musk waited too long to bring his case, and the statute of limitations has expired.
Chander said he already plans to discuss the trial when he teaches his contracts course in the fall to illustrate the perils of resting millions of dollars on a handshake deal. “I think this is all going to be new legal ground,” he said. “It’s going to be taught in casebooks in the future.”

标题: Should AI and Christianity Mix? Pat Gelsinger and Gloo Holdings Sure Think So
内容:
Last year, Pat Gelsinger found himself pondering a thorny, existential question: What should he do with his life after Intel?
The chipmaker
had unceremoniously ousted
him in December 2024, and shortly after, his wife Linda gave him a firm nudge. “You’re not done yet,” she told him, as Gelsinger recalls it. So Gelsinger considered roles in government and private equity. Neither seemed right. Instead, he decided to embark on an unusual crusade by taking up a post as executive chairman and head of technology at Gloo Holdings Inc., a Boulder, Colo.-based company that sells AI products and other software. He’s the person responsible for developing the tech that the publicly traded company hopes to sell to every church, congregation and Christian-minded company it possibly can.
“This genie isn’t going back in the bottle,” said Gelsinger, 65. “The AI models get embedded into everything going forward.” And as a result, “we need those to be good,” he said—as in, morally good, not just good, sophisticated pieces of software.
“They need to reflect who we are as a people, as humans—as creations in God’s image,” Gelsinger said.
Pat Gelsinger speaks at a 2024 Intel event in Tawian. (Getty Images)
Gloo’s corporate proselytizing is gaining some momentum. The company expects to reach $190 million in revenue this year, up from $90 million last year, and more than 140,000 churches and ministry leaders already use its dozens of wide-ranging products. Among them is a subscription-based web platform for pastors and anyone else managing a church that includes an AI chatbot that drafts sermons, schedules volunteers and writes personalized messages to congregants. The platform will also automatically chop up lengthy sermons into captioned clips optimized for social media, ranking the clips in its library based on their potential to go viral. (Gloo offers a free version that contains a few of the programs, along with several subscription tiers that provide additional features.)
For organizations like universities, nonprofits and businesses that want to develop their own AI products, Gloo sells a product, Gloo AI Studio, which offers versions of popular large language models that the company says it has trained to better adhere to Christian values. What’s the result? Well, ask a model that has been modified with Gloo’s technology about the purpose of life, and depending on the model it may respond thusly: “to glorify God.” At the moment, Gloo AI Studio is used by companies like the Waco, Texas-based LYTE, whose daily devotional app was built using the technology.
Gelsinger’s public embrace of his new role at Gloo coincides with a broader religious resurgence in Silicon Valley. For decades, as he rose through the ranks at Intel and then the software giant VMware, Gelsinger was one of few openly Christian executives in Silicon Valley and would regularly post Bible verses on X. He seemed like an oddity: At the time, technology and religion were seen as unmixable forces. But in recent years, that perception has softened, with a growing number of the
tech elite
publicly
embracing their faith
.
With Gelsinger and Scott Beck, the company’s cofounder and CEO, at the helm, Gloo takes a maximalist approach to melding AI and Christianity together. More broadly, though, the religion’s faithful remain divided over how to approach AI—a tension reflected in Gloo’s own data. A February
study
by Barna Group, a research firm owned by Gloo, found that under a third of U.S. adults trust spiritual guidance from AI the way they would from a pastor. Meanwhile, Pope Leo XIV
reportedly
warned clergy at a recent meeting against the “temptation” of using AI to write sermons, arguing that large language models cannot replicate genuine faith.
Clearly, Gloo’s path forward isn’t totally rosy. Since going public in November, its stock has dropped around 14%, while the Nasdaq has gone up about 10%. It also remains deeply in the red, posting a $159 million net loss last year, up from $86 million in 2024. Beck and Gelsinger hope to eke out a profit this year, which would ease the worries that hang on the stock, said Yun Kim, a Loop Capital analyst who covers the company.
“The No. 1 focus for all the investors is [whether] they’ll reach profitability by the fourth quarter,” he said. “That’s the No. 1 catalyst for the stock.”
Beyond just pushing its own AI products, Gloo is also trying to change what other large AI companies are selling. Over the past year, the company has developed a system for evaluating major models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google and other firms based on how they promote faith and “human flourishing,” a term originally coined in Harvard University research that sought to quantify the pillars of a happy and fulfilled life. (The company is interested in this non-religious metric because some of its customers are nonprofits focused on causes like homelessness or poverty, rather than Christian organizations.) To assemble the system, Gelsinger and Gloo partnered with Valkyrie, an Austin,Texas-based AI startup, and Biblica, a nonprofit focused on Bible translations, to compile more than 1,200 questions that spanned faith, finance and relationships, among other topics. They then tested nearly 30 models against them. While some performed well on more general questions related to “character” or “health,” they all failed on the subject of “faith,” according to Gloo’s analysis.
Gelsinger has been presenting this research to the leaders of AI labs, whom he thinks should present options that allow users to refine AI models to reflect a “Christian worldview.” He would not name the companies he’s met with. Those conversations are ongoing.
Gloo sees an opening to win converts to its mission in Silicon Valley. Last October, the company opened a Palo Alto office, in part to help draw more tech talent. Gelsinger recently bought a condo a few minutes walk away where he stays during the week. His main home is in Sonoma. The condo is also close to the office of Playground, a venture capital firm where Gelsinger is a general partner.
“I’m creating the perfect life,” he said. “If I want to see my wife, I know where she is: two hours away in wine country.”
Gelsinger’s arrival has helped breathe new life into 13-year-old Gloo, which has lacked a clear direction in the past and has also previously been caught up in controversy.
The company was founded in 2013 by Beck and his wife, Theresa. Beck previously founded Einstein Bros. Bagels and held executive positions at multiple large consumer brands, including Blockbuster and Boston Market. On the side, he said he and Theresa would fund campus and marriage ministries through their family office. That’s where Beck spotted an opportunity. Many of the ministries were still using bulletin boards and other outdated tools to communicate with their congregants. He wanted them to “get the benefit of the next generation of technologies,” he said.
The company’s use of that technology in a religious context was sometimes controversial. According to a Wall Street Journal
report
, the company used a mix of third-party and church-supplied data to help churches target ads to people experiencing mental health concerns and personal crises, like marital problems: Gloo figured they’d be likely to seek comfort by joining a congregation. In marketing materials, Gloo claimed to have “thousands of data attributes” on 245 million people in the U.S.
A spokesperson for Gloo said the company discontinued this product, Gloo Insights+, in early 2021, three years after it was introduced. The spokesperson went on to stress that Gloo is “not a data mining company.”
Advertising and marketing do remain a major part of Gloo’s business, accounting for roughly half of Gloo’s overall revenue. Most of that comes from Masterworks, an ads and fundraising platform used by churches and larger Christian organizations to reach possible donors.
Masterworks was purchased by Gloo last July, and its former CEO, Bryan Brown, now runs
Gloo’s entire advertising business. The company has “specialized in helping figure out where Christians go” online, he said, which has allowed Gloo to create a workaround for strict data privacy laws that ban targeting users by religion. Instead of tracking individuals, they buy ad inventory on platforms Christians frequent, such as Christian publications and radio stations, reaching the audience without violating sensitive data protections, according to Brown.
Masterworks is part of Gloo’s recent M&A binge. Since 2023, it has bought 18 companies, including Church Law & Tax, which sells a web platform to churches for managing legal and financial matters; Church Metrics, an analytics tool for tracking church attendance; and Outreach, a Colorado Springs, Colorado-based outfit that sells church accouterments, like banners, signs and chalice communion cups.
Pope Leo XIV has spoken out against AI and using it in religious services. (Getty Images)
All these acquisitions have helped boost Gloo’s revenue. But they were costly, and the company is now laser-focused on reducing expenses and reaching profitability by the end of the year, according to Gelsinger. Going public last year helped Gloo restructure a large portion of the roughly $180 million in debt it had pre-IPO. (Gloo raised $73 million from its public offering and converted about $140 million of its debt into equity.)
Still, the company seems far from making money. To that end, Gloo conducted layoffs of its staff in December and January, and both Gelsinger and Beck cut their salaries to $1. For the former Intel CEO, the experience inspired some déjà vu. “I did this at Intel at one point as well,” Gelsinger said. “If we don’t make those hard trade-offs, get to profitability, get to sustainable growth, then we can’t do anything going forward.”
Last week, Gelsinger took Gloo’s pitch out to a like-minded audience at the Missional AI Global Summit, which convened in a Marriott ballroom down in Santa Clara just as HumanX, the giant AI conference catering to the industry’s mainstream, was getting underway in San Francisco. The Missional AI event, which began in 2019, bills itself as the premier gathering for Christian leaders, technologists and AI professionals and has ballooned in popularity recently.
The event had a jubilant air. To open the event, Eric Célérier, founder of HelloBible, a France-based AI chatbot for Bible questions, led the crowd in the Lord’s Prayer and recounted how he kissed the floor upon landing at the Santa Clara airport, saying he “finally felt at home.”
Throughout the day, Christian leaders and entrepreneurs showcased how AI is reshaping their work—from translating TV shows into hundreds of languages to driving record downloads of their apps. Bobby Gruenewald, founder and CEO of YouVersion, whose Bible App has been downloaded more than 700 million times from the Apple App Store, said he personally uses one billion tokens a week and 16 agents.
During on-stage remarks, Gruenewald mentioned that he was recently asked about whether he ever confuses the Holy Spirit with ChatGPT—especially when God takes a long time to respond and a chatbot provides an answer in seconds. At least for the time being, Gruenewald shrugged off the possibility. AI “has use cases that are incredible but it also has limitations,” he said.
When I asked Gelsinger about this, he was less convinced. He believes AI chatbots will be used for prayer and said he already sometimes uses the technology to do so.
He concedes that AI isn’t perfect. “Am I concerned that people could use this tool inappropriately? Very much so. But to say, ‘Don’t use the tool.’ To me, that’s like, ‘Don’t use a word processor—we should go back to chisel and quills,’” Gelsinger said. “It will be used, and now the question is, ‘How can we make sure it’s used well?’”

标题: Microsoft Tightens Grip on GPUs, Pressuring AI Customers
内容:
AI startups are struggling to access
Nvidia
graphics processing units as
Microsoft
and other cloud providers divert GPU stockpiles to their internal teams or bigger cloud customers, leaving smaller firms scrambling for the remaining servers at higher prices.
The supply crunch is impacting well-funded AI startups that have raised money from Sequoia Capital, Founders Fund, General Catalyst and Andreessen Horowitz and other major investment firms, according to founders and investors at some of those firms. The shortage prompted General Catalyst’s Hemant Taneja to send a survey to founders, inquiring about their ability to access compute, according to a person with direct knowledge.
“We’ve heard from many of you that compute—and GPU access in particular—is one of the biggest bottlenecks you’re facing this year,” Taneja wrote.
The dynamic is
reminiscent of early 2023
, when cloud providers clawed back capacity from their cloud services to support their internal teams and a few key customers, such as OpenAI. Venture firms including Andreessen and Index Ventures ended up securing their own pools of GPUs to ease the crunch for their portfolio companies.
But unlike that period, when AI applications were nascent, exceptional demand for AI coding tools is exacerbating the current shortage. Cloud providers are tightening GPU capacity for smaller customers as demand surges from large AI developers such as Anthropic, as well as for coding and other automation tools, according to executives at the cloud firms and startup founders.
This time around, General Catalyst is working on a way to help its portfolio companies access GPUs, potentially by facilitating a shared pool of capacity or directly negotiating on a startup’s behalf.
The chip shortage is allowing cloud firms to raise prices they charge to rent Nvidia-powered servers. That’s providing a much-needed boost to cloud provider margins, after some recently struggled to make money on the chips.
But the higher prices are felt by startups like Krea, which develops image-generating AI models. The four-year-old startup has raised $83 million in funding from investors including Andreessen and Bain Capital Ventures.
Six months ago, Krea signed a six-month contract to rent several hundred Nvidia Blackwell chips for $2.80 an hour per chip after several cloud providers competed aggressively for the startup’s business. But in the last month, when the startup began looking for more AI servers to train a model from scratch, some sales representatives at cloud providers wouldn’t pick up the phone, said co-founder and CEO Victor Perez.
When sales representatives finally did return their calls, they told him prices had increased significantly and they wouldn’t engage with him unless he signed a three-year commitment.
“Some ghosted, some told us no availability, others tried to get us into crazy bad deals,” Perez said.
And while Krea was evaluating offers for some clusters, those clusters ended up getting scooped up by other customers within days, he said.
In the end, the founders reached an agreement to pay $3.70 an hour for another several hundred Blackwell chips in a one-year commitment—32% higher than their last deal. And even that price seemed cheap, given the chip prices he had seen from other providers.
“The biggest fear for us is to not have compute where we can run our platform and run models where we want to train,” Perez said. “If the price gets a little higher, it’s not going to kill us.”
In another example, a startup founder seeking to rent a tightly connected cluster of nearly 1,000 GPUs said an Nvidia salesperson told them last week that it would be difficult to find such a cluster at the largest cloud providers, given the number of customers seeking chips in even greater numbers. The founder said they are still looking for a cluster, which would cost more than $70,000 per day to rent.
Contract Renewals
Making matters worse for startups is that Microsoft, Amazon, CoreWeave and other cloud providers are making ever-bigger multibillion-dollar commitments to provide large numbers of GPUs to Anthropic and OpenAI. (Those commitments
haven’t been enough to stave off a compute shortage at Anthropic
as it experiences an unprecedented growth spurt.)
Another reason for the shortage: Many AI startups previously struck two- or three-year cloud deals that are now coming to an end, giving cloud providers an opportunity to sell them higher-priced deals or reallocate that capacity to other parties.
For example, one CEO of an AI cloud provider said they recently planned to shift one customer’s GPU cluster to another customer that was willing to pay around 30% more because the original customer’s contract was up for renewal. But after the original customer pleaded to keep their chips, the cloud provider ended up letting them keep the GPUs at the higher price.
GPU cloud provider Lightning AI, meanwhile, has around 40,000 GPUs online but a backlog of potential rental orders from roughly 40 customers seeking about 400,000 GPUs, CEO Will Falcon said. That’s driven up prices more than 25% in the last six months, Falcon said, from around $1.60 per hour per chip to more than $2 now and even higher in some cases. (Most of Lightning’s chips are Hoppers, an older generation of Nvidia chips.)
Microsoft’s Use ’Em or Lose ’Em Policy
At Microsoft, demand from large customers and internal Microsoft teams prompted the company’s Azure cloud group to limit how many servers it is willing to rent to smaller customers, according to a Microsoft employee with direct knowledge. Some of the smaller customers are already facing months-long wait times to rent additional GPUs, this person said.
Azure sales leaders have recently told staff that customers should expect long wait times to persist at least through the end of 2026, this person said.
Microsoft has long reserved its largest clusters of cutting-edge chips for OpenAI and its own internal use, and is also building new clusters for Anthropic. For other Azure customers, getting access to GPUs depends on how much money they’re already spending on Azure cloud services and how much additional cash they’re willing to commit to the AI servers.
For instance, Microsoft in recent months began asking customers that want access to Nvidia Blackwell chips to commit to renting at least 1,000 of the chips for at least a year, a contract that costs tens of millions of dollars at a minimum, according to the Microsoft employee with direct knowledge.
Customers must wait weeks or months to rent even a small number of older generations of Nvidia chips on Azure, according to the Microsoft employee and a customer that has tried reserving chips.
The length of the wait time depends on customers’ existing relationship with Azure, which prioritizes customers based on a tiered system, the Microsoft employee said. Tier 1 customers who get priority access include roughly 1,000 of its biggest cloud spenders while Tier 2 customers are smaller spenders but still big enough that Microsoft assigns dedicated sales representatives to manage their counts. Tier 3 customers are smaller businesses whose relationship is managed by one of Microsoft’s reseller partners, such as CDW.
Customers that don’t commit to reserving a large number of GPUs face long wait times to rent the chips on a pay-as-you-go basis. After Microsoft gives them GPU capacity on that basis, it tracks how much they’re using the GPUs and may remove their access if they let the servers stay idle for even a few hours, the Microsoft employee said.
Similarly, Microsoft has been rescinding GPU access for some companies that got access to the chips through Microsoft for Startups, a program that gives startups free credits to rent servers. Microsoft has told such startups that they will lose access to GPUs if they aren’t fully utilizing the chips, the Microsoft employee said.
Going Direct?
Some startup founders might bypass the cloud providers altogether. Collin McLelland, founder of the startup Collide, which raised a $14 million seed round last year and is developing AI agents for oil and gas companies, said his company is considering spending around $500,000 to buy Nvidia GPUs to run on their own because he got fed up with long wait times and constraints of renting GPUs from large cloud providers. Collide is considering leasing space directly from a data center firm or a cloud provider to host the GPUs after receiving them, he said.
While buying and installing the GPUs is significantly more expensive in the short term than renting them from a cloud provider, McLelland said, he determined it was worth doing to avoid delays and uncertainty around the rentals, and expects to spend less on the GPUs compared to renting them over several years.
“It’s a huge risk for us to not have any compute when we need it,” he said. “Most people are just scared of hardware. I’ve owned oil wells so I’m numb to it.”

标题: Benson Boone, Tech’s Go-To Performer
内容:
Welcome, Weekenders!
In this newsletter:
•
The Big Read:
The tech elite
are using AI to supercharge
their cancer battles
•
Artificial Intelligence:
Should
AI and Christianity
mix?
•
Tech Culture:
Amid AI backlash, billionaires
demand new breed
of bodyguard—plus, inside the star-studded
Breakthrough Prize gala
•
Plus, Recommendations—our weekly pop culture picks:
“
Family Lore
,” “
The Long Run
” and “
Scream 7
”
Let’s get straight
to one of the biggest happenings at Google Cloud Next, the company’s annual cloud conference: singer Benson Boone’s performance, which closed out the evening on Thursday. Boone gave the crowd what they wanted—two backflips, two frontflips and one roundoff back handspring situation. Every time he climbed on the piano, people started chanting: “Backflip! Backflip! Backflip!”
Boone, a mustachioed 23-year-old with a mop of floppy Zoomer hair and biceps visible from the nosebleed seats, is as famous for those acrobatics as he is for his treacly, earwormy pop songs. Together, they’ve made him an inescapable cultural presence over the past year—at least for certain demographics. One such group: teen girls on TikTok. Another is the tech elite.
Last February, Mark Zuckerberg, for instance,
performed
as Boone at his wife Priscilla Chan’s 40th birthday party, clad in the same open-to-the-navel blue jumpsuit that the pop star donned
in his Grammy performance
. (Rolling Stone’s
headline
: “Mark Zuckerberg Does Benson Boone Cosplay Nobody Asked For.”)
In the fall, Boone performed at Dreamforce, Marc Benioff’s festival celebrating customer-relationship software, clogged San Francisco streets and the aggressive importation of Hawaiian culture.
And then there Boone was at Google Cloud Next at Allegiant Stadium in Las Vegas, with Weezer as his opening act.
Why is tech so Boone-pilled? I posed the question to Zuckerberg, Benioff and Google Cloud, all of whom addressed it with the same secrecy as their AI model weights. In an email, a spokesperson for Zuckerberg said: “Going to pass on participating but such a cute story.” Later, my colleague Laura Bratton texted Benioff on my behalf and asked him why he’d gotten Boone for Dreamforce. He replied simply by sending a picture of himself standing next to Boone.
Then I went to Google. Does Thomas Kurian, the company’s cloud chief, have a favorite Benson Boone song? Did Google decide to book Boone as part of its rivalry with Zuckerberg’s Meta Platforms? All a spokesperson would say is that Boone felt like “a natural fit for our community” and noted that he has previously appeared at YouTube events.
On the floor at Allegiant Stadium, with thousands of agentic AI’s most fervent—or reluctant—Boone viewers, Joanna Duff, 45, a marketing associate director at Accenture, diagnosed him with having a certain appeal. “What is it the kids say, ‘rizz’?” she asked.
Matt Hull, a Google vice president, had worked his way to the barricades to FaceTime his 11-year-old daughter. He didn’t care about Boone himself, he said. Then he pointed to his phone’s screen, where I could see his daughter dancing joyously at home. “That’s what it’s all about,” Hull said.
Meanwhile, Bentley, a 22-year-old who would only tell me her first name, was sobbing after the concert wrapped, overwhelmed by the opportunity to see Boone. She said she had initially discovered his music at the end of high school. “I’m the biggest fan,” said Bentley. She was lucky enough to walk away with a memorable souvenir: a towel used by Boone to wipe his face, which a stagehand gave her. “I stood out because there were a lot of old men in the front.”
On Friday, I returned to San Francisco, the land of college dropouts and
20-something billionaire founders
, and I thought: Boone, in many ways, is not so dissimilar from how our technologists like to perceive themselves. Tech has always loved a 23-year-old with stage presence: fresh-faced and affable, oozing showbiz and
literally draped in the American flag
. So what if his PR team ignored my request to learn his favorite AI models? At least, he can do a backflip.
—Erin Woo (
erin@theinformation.com
)
What else from this week…
• A tiiiiny bit of news from Cupertino: Tim Cook is leaving Apple, and his departure has prompted a lot of takes on his legacy: Tim Cook,
cash machine
; Tim Cook,
subscriptions czar
; Tim Cook,
chief AirPodder
; and Tim Cook,
empire builder
, among many others.
• Sullivan & Cromwell, one of the more prominent white-shoe law firms,
had to fess up in court
that a legal document was full of AI hallucinations.
• At this very moment, the crooks behind the largest-ever crypto heist
are quite actively
laundering their ill-gotten gains.
• Resurrection is, in fact, possible—at least in the hands of data-recovery specialists, who are in demand enough to merit
their own New Yorker story
.
• Ever wonder what Jeff Bezos’ annual Campfire microsummit is like? Hollywood director Noah Hawley
found out
when he got an invite a few years ago, seemingly an attempt to woo him away from Disney. (Speaking of microsummits, more on how that type of social gathering has gotten popular among the tech elite
in this Weekend story
from last year.)
• Is Elon Musk
really the John Rockefeller
of the future?
• As often seems to happen when YouTubers and podcasters try their hand at mass expansion, things
are not going very well
for Alex Cooper of “Call Her Daddy” fame.
—Abram Brown (
abe@theinformation.com
)

Chatbots are helping techies
come up with daring treatment plans
for themselves or loved ones facing dire medical diagnoses. But those plans can be risky and cost hundreds of thousands of dollars to put into action.

Should AI and Christianity Mix? Pat Gelsinger and Gloo Holdings Sure Think So
The ex-Intel CEO
is helping lead

Tech Elite Embrace New Breed of Bodyguards After Altman Attack, AI Backlash
The technorati are unsettled. And as business booms for bodyguard firms, the wealthy
want their protectors
to be smarter—and chummier—than the old school muscled-up goons.
Tech Culture
The Night Hollywood and Silicon Valley Celebrated Together
Inside
the Breakthrough Prize gala
, the annual, star-studded occasion that marks the world’s most significant scientific achievements.
Listening:
“
Family Lore
”
Lore is quite like trauma: Every family has some. Mine has a story about a great-grandmother who once performed with Annie Oakley and another about a grandfather from Canada who worked as a carnie and moonlighted as a bootlegger. (Unsurprisingly, he is also a source of a great deal of trauma.) In “Family Lore,” host Lloyd Lochridge sets out to unravel the mystery behind various pseudo-myths handed down in clans across America. Lochridge, a podcast veteran and former documentary producer of series like ESPN’s 20-hour “Basketball: A Love Story,” is an able, amiable detective, generally finding that many supposed truths are at least semi-real. Take the legend passed along in the family of Martha Sayers, of Laredo, Texas, the one about her beautiful Aunt Margherita being the person who turned a concoction of lime juice, orange liqueur and tequila into, yes, the Margarita cocktail. Turns out, it’s mostly true.
—Abram Brown
Reading:
“
The Long Run
” by Martin Dugard
I enjoy running, but I’ve never wanted to do a marathon. I suppose it’s for the same reason that while I’ve happily dated many men one at a time, I’ve never had any interest in polyamory: All good things are best enjoyed in moderation. Still, I'm an exception to the general cultural norm these days—especially when it comes to marathons. In the past several decades, these 26.2-mile quests have become a mainstream hobby; fueled an ever-burgeoning and increasingly high-tech business of running apparel; and given birth to major tourist draws for cities worldwide. Here in New York, for instance, almost as many people turn out on the first Sunday in November to watch the New York City Marathon as they do on the third Thursday of the month to see the Macy’s Thanksgiving Day Parade.
As one race organizer tells author Martin Dugard, a longtime correspondent for publications like Runner’s World, a marathon is today “the great suburban Everest,” a totally achievable bucket-list goal held by millions of people.
“Running has always been a low-cost way to exercise—no balls, no sticks, you can do it naked if you like,” notes Dugard, himself a passionate former marathoner. (The Greeks went au naturel, as he notes, as regularly do some folks in San Francisco’s annual Bay to Breakers 12K.) “But for years it languished in the deep and lonely bunker of cult status.” With admirable fleet-footedness, Dugard charts how a “collision of events, gold medals, legislation and popular culture” have produced “a movement radically altering not just how we approach fitness but our lifestyles and communication and good health.”
—A.B.
Watching:
“
Scream 7
”
“Scream 7” reconfirms an existential fact about Hollywood: The industry is deeply freaked out about AI, an honest-to-goodness subplot within this movie. Amid the technology’s proliferation, the usual anxiety around career longevity among entertainment’s old hands has deepened; writer-director Kevin Williamson, 61, who has penned three other “Scream” films, including the original, makes the worry plainly apparent here in the series’ latest installment.
But part of the fun of the “Scream” franchise has always been to watch the zeitgeist and cultural tropes of the moment get splattered around. And besides, “Scream 7” is just a good ol’ smirky, bloody romp, with Sidney Prescott (Neve Campbell) once again at the center of things, pulling her daughter Tatum (Isabel May) into the ever-present danger of attracting masked, knife-wielding looneys. Of course, Gale Weathers (Courteney Cox) shows up, trailed by a pair of young survivors from previous Ghostface encounters who’ve become eager apprentices (Mason Gooding, Jasmin Savoy Brown). As they try to parse the connection between digital bogeymen and IRL corpses, I especially enjoyed a gunshot-loud comment on teens and their AirPods.
—A.B.

标题: The Night Hollywood and Silicon Valley Celebrated Together
内容:
In a festive merger, the glitterati and the technorati combined forces in Los Angeles a week ago for the annual Breakthrough Prize black-tie gala, which hands out million-dollar awards to the world’s leading scientists.
The shiniest names of Hollywood attended, including Ben Affleck, Margot Robbie, Zoe Saldaña and Oscar Isaac. Silicon Valley’s who-who turned out, too, a contingent lead by the likes of Elon Musk, Sam Altman, Google’s Sundar Pichai and Michael Dell, among many others. The prizes are the brainchild of a group of the tech elite led by investor Yuri Milner, founder of DST Global.
This year’s accolades went to researchers who’ve made great leaps forward in physics, gene therapies and other topics. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang presented a new award, the Vera Rubin New Frontiers Prize, that honors women physicists who’ve recently finished their doctorates. The award is named after astronomer Vera Rubin, who is also the namesake of Nvidia’s new line of AI chips.
While accepting an award, mathematician Frank Merle cast the quest that underpinned his efforts in lofty terms. “Great science can be a game you play with gods,” he said, “where every gain is a spiritual delicacy.”

标题: Silicon Valley Embraces New Breed of Bodyguards After Altman Attack, AI Backlash
内容:
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang cuts a distinctive image, habitually clad in one of his many leather jackets. But it wasn’t Huang’s outfit that caught the attention of another tech founder who saw him in Manhattan last year: it was the phalanx of security guards walking behind him.
“He had a posse,” said the founder, who estimates Huang had at least five people in his detail.
It’s not just Huang. This founder said he has also noticed increased security around others such Databricks’ Ali Ghodsi and Shopify’s Tobi Lütke at industry events. Their bulked-up protection has made the founder—who would only speak on the condition of anonymity, lest he raise worrisome attention on himself—wonder if he should increase his security, too. (Neither Nvidia, Databricks nor Shopify would comment for this story.)
There was once a time in Silicon Valley when even its richest and most famous denizens eschewed personal security—or at most opted for a bodyguard who might also double as a driver. But times have changed, and security concerns are increasingly top of mind for the tech elite. The worries began to mount after UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson’s assassination in 2024, and they have escalated further as public sentiment has turned darkly negative on AI. This led to a dramatic incident a few weeks ago when someone hurled a Molotov cocktail at Sam Altman’s house in San Francisco before smashing a door at OpenAI headquarters.
Conversations with a dozen executive security outfits, tech executives, wealth managers and other insiders showed the attacks have underscored a new cultural paradigm: the growing sense that to be wealthy and prominent in tech brings real risk of being targeted. As such, a new embrace of security by the technorati is emerging.
James Hamilton, a former FBI special agent who started Hamilton Security Group after cutting his teeth with Gavin de Becker—a longtime security officer for Jeff Bezos as well as celebrities like Cher and Madonna—said the demand for his services is up tenfold since his company opened two years ago.
“It’s very, very high,” said Hamilton, who lives in South Carolina but travels around the world for work. “We’re getting consultation requests, training requests, personnel requests, travel requests, risk-assessment requests.”
Data crunched by analytics firm Equilar
show that
more than a third of S&P 500 companies provided security perks for executives in 2025—more than double the 12.8% from the year before. And the median cost of these security packages rose 20% year over year.
Even a basic round-the-clock detail of two to three guards can run $500,000 or more a year—while a larger package of residential and travel teams can cost well north of $10 million. For companies, there is a tax benefit to lessen the expense: as long as a third-party firm confirms the risk to the executive, companies can write it off on their taxes.
Among the big tech firms, Meta Platforms has long shelled out the most on security, spending an eye-popping $22 million on protection for Mark Zuckerberg and his family in 2025 and $24 million the year before that. That is more than Google, Apple, Amazon and Netflix combined, according to SEC filings. Among those four companies, Google spends the most, plunking down $8.2 million on Sundar Pichai’s security in 2024, according
to the most recent disclosures
. (Many other publicly traded companies don’t disclose security spending.)
As for Nvidia, Huang’s own security costs swelled from $690,000 in 2023 to
$3.5 million last year
, according to its financial disclosures.
Multiple people said that security was becoming a more visible part of life in elite circles. One founder gave the example of seeing people at Semafor’s World Economy summit of CEOs and political leaders with “Amazon AP” on conference badges in place of their names. The mysterious people told him they were in “asset protection,” there, seemingly, to watch over an Amazon vice president, Jason Buechel, who runs Whole Foods. (Amazon declined to comment.)
***
The embrace of security in Silicon Valley marks a change from the industry’s origins, when many founders worked hard to maintain an identity as regular people—self-made entrepreneurs who still wanted to be able to just hop in an Uber by themselves or go for a walk without a crowd.
As a result, the Valley is full of stories about reluctant CEOs who took on security only after pressure from company boards. Jack Dorsey, for instance, had to be cajoled by the directors of both Square and Twitter to take on security protection after
announcing plans
to decamp to Africa for half the year in 2020, said a former Square board member.
“We were like, ‘Dude, you’re not going to Africa without security. Somebody’s going to kidnap a billionaire over there,’” the former board member recalled.
And though Elon Musk has recently become known for his in-your-face security measures—
motorcades
and a traveling detail of 20 people—he
reportedly
first added personal security more than 10 years ago only after Tesla’s board required it.
Morgan Stevens, a former British intelligence officer and global head of security at Crisis24, which provides security and guards for tech VIPs, recalled how a longtime client of his, a founder, once pointed out Musk’s oversized detail at a conference and said, “Never do I want to be that person.” That same person used to ditch Stevens by leaving the house surreptitiously. But now many years later, the client has embraced his security coverage, speaking in interviews about how important his security team is to him.
Crisis24 represents nine out of the 15 wealthiest Americans—about half of whom work in tech—and more than half of the world’s largest 500 companies, Stevens said. The firm can barely keep up with demand these days. The company, which purchased 60% of de Becker’s firm in 2021, has expanded the group from 350 security agents to more than 2,000 since then.
Even companies with smaller profiles than the Teslas of the world have been bulking up. Will DeWitt, a former Marine sergeant who heads security for the
Sequoia Capital-backed security camera startup Verkada
, says his department has more than doubled in size since he started in 2023. The company said through a spokesperson that it has also increased security measures for CEO Filip Kaliszan, but declined to specify more.
“You want to build these programs before you need them,” said DeWitt, who used to run security for Palantir. “People are starting to be more proactive instead of waiting until an incident happens.”
In the social media age, threats materialize with more speed and complexity, said Stevens.
“It used to take weeks and months for folks to grow aggrieved by someone’s position,” he said. “That now can just be days. It can be as simple as someone being pissed off about a post.”
Crisis24 and other companies also devote increasingly large amounts of time to collecting digital intelligence. Their analysts often set up what are called geofences to monitor social media communications near locations where protectees are scheduled for appearances. Problematic tweets, posts and rants are fed back to private security and sometimes law enforcement on the ground.
The volume of online discourse and content means there is more information at everyone’s fingertips: a boon for security teams who want to foil a plot but also for people intent on wreaking havoc in a world where a single Instagram snap at the beach or a concert can inadvertently leak critical personal information. Multiple security firms offer training to wealthy families about their social media activity and digital habits.
Executives in the AI industry,
which is currently polling worse than ICE
, are keenly aware of the threats, experts said. The cryptocurrency world is another pocket of alarm after a series of high-profile, crypto-related kidnappings, including
a headline-grabbing attack
in San Francisco last fall and
an even more gruesome one
in New York earlier in the year. Now, insurance startups like Relm are eager to market kidnapping insurance and other types of policies to the crypto rich.
“People target crypto because you can get large amounts of money and you can walk away with the claim,” said Mitchell Amador, CEO of blockchain security company Immunefi. “It’s a thieves’ utopia.”
Private security executives said the old paradigm of the earpiece wearing guard in shades and a suit is outdated—too on-the-nose for some founders these days. Instead, many people are asking for agents who fit into their professional and personal lives in appearance and demeanor. That means casual outfits when the occasion calls for it and a more sophisticated bearing.
“We want someone who can blend into the board meeting and then take them to dinner afterward,” Stevens said, noting that the job has expanded over the years from being a bodyman to serving as a client’s confidante and companion. He pointed to his years of work with the well-known tech founder in the Bay Area.
“Him and I were on a walk last week and we were discussing different colors for the backsplashes in his kitchen,” he said. “It really becomes a relationship where you become a trusted advisor whether it is about their kitchen, or safety and security.”

标题: Can AI Help a Tech CEO Cure His Spouse’s Brain Cancer?
内容:
When Alicia Kalogeropoulos was first diagnosed in 2018 with brain cancer, she underwent surgery, and three years later, she enrolled in a clinical trial for a pill that worked to keep her disease in check for years.
In February, Alicia and her husband Alex received grim news: Her tumor had returned. Her physicians at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center in New York advised Alicia, 35, that her options were another surgery, likely followed by chemotherapy and radiation. Alex knew from his research that the doctors’ plan wasn’t a cure. So he asked AI chatbots to weigh in.
“I want you to come up with a treatment plan for Alicia,” he instructed ChatGPT. “I want to focus on treatments that do not destroy her immune system efficacy and function. I want you to utilize all available tools and all available pathways we could attack this tumor.”
Alex, 35, was already using AI constantly for his job. He is the CEO and founder of The Social Station, a tech company in Allentown, Pa., that helps hospitality businesses manage their social media presence. While plenty of people regularly consult chatbots for medical advice, he viewed AI as a way to go beyond ordinary patient advocacy. And he aimed to quickly turn himself into a kind of medical expert who could help push the envelope of his wife’s care.
For decades, people confronted with dire medical prognoses for themselves or their loved ones have been drivers of citizen science. That term is often used to describe situations involving patients who, lacking good conventional treatment options, drive the development of new therapies, cutting-edge research and novel partnerships with scientists to help themselves and others.
In the pre-AI era, citizen scientists tracked down doctors on the Internet, read scientific papers online and set up groups on Facebook to share their experiences with fellow patients. Some devised and ran their own scientific experiments, looking for leads in shared consumer DNA tests results, blood tests or from symptom trackers.
From left: Alex Kalogeropoulos, his wife Alicia and their daughter Kora on a cruise ship in the Caribbean in 2025. Courtesy of Alex Kalogeropoulos
The rise of AI, though, has supercharged the phenomenon. AI can examine more research papers and data than any patient or doctor ever could hope to, which is especially critical in rare diseases where data are usually sparse and there are fewer medical experts. When existing treatments fail, AI can suggest strategies grounded in scientific reasoning that go beyond the medical consensus for how to treat a disease, known as standard of care.
Or at least that’s the hope. Even if AI searches every scrap of available information about complicated diseases like cancer, so much is still unknown about them. “Everybody expects AI to deliver drugs tomorrow,” said Noubar Afeyan, co-founder and chair of Moderna, the drug company, and founder and CEO of Flagship Pioneering, a venture capital firm. “That’s not a realistic expectation.”
Doing the laborious work to develop the technology to produce mRNA cancer vaccines, for example, cost Moderna hundreds of millions of dollars, Afeyan estimated. “That capability is not available online for an AI model that scrapes the public literature to do,” he said.
And there’s a risk that untested drugs can fail—or worse.
“Most good ideas turn out to not be true: You only find out when you put a drug in a person, and there is a toxicity,” said Christopher Austin, a senior vice president of research and development technologies at the pharmaceutical company GSK, who used to work closely with citizen scientists trying treat their conditions when he was at the National Institutes of Health.
Along with AI, citizen science is booming in Silicon Valley. In March, Australian tech entrepreneur Paul Conyngham
went viral with an account
of how he had used AI to design a personalized cancer vaccine for his dog, Rosie, which he credited with helping shrink her tumors and extending her life. “The cancer right now is relatively under control,” he said.
And Sid Sijbrandij, co-founder and executive chair of GitLab, the software development platform, gave a presentation at a major cancer society meeting earlier this month about his own experience using AI to research novel treatments for his rare bone cancer. He also went on an OpenAI podcast to talk about it.
Sijbrandij’s osteosarcoma was in remission until 2024 when a scan showed the disease was progressing. Sijbrandij had the kind of medical help many can only dream of, which included a team of seven doctors, among them a leading osteosarcoma expert.
But he told me AI had given him options his doctors hadn’t suggested. His doctors, bound by professional and ethical codes, wanted to try one drug at a time to make sure Sijbrandij didn’t have a life-threatening reaction and also to determine if those drugs were actually working, he believed.
AI analyzed the scientific literature and suggested ways for Sijbrandij and his team to combine the personalized medicines Sijbrandij was pursuing.
“Your doctor has limited time to do research for you. Even if AI is not perfect, they are full time on your case,” Sijbrandij said.
His doctors approved the treatment decisions, but with AI, he said, “You flip the script to the doctor having to say it’s a bad idea. A lot of things they wouldn’t suggest but are not bad ideas.”
While big companies like OpenAI and Anthropic are starting to heavily invest in building AI models for biology and other parts of science, some citizen scientists are also turning their own experiences into business ideas. Sijbrandij used his own money to start Even One Ventures last year to invest in personalized health and precision medicine companies. One of them, Valius Sciences, does advanced diagnostics on hard-to-treat cancers and just announced it raised another $10 million in additional capital, including some from Joe Lonsdale’s 8VC.
Conyngham, who made a vaccine for his dog, was recently in the San Francisco Bay Area raising money to start a company to offer the same service to other dog owners. OpenAI’s Sam Altman was among those who
said they met
with him.
“We want to start in dogs but hope to eventually help people,” Conyngham told me.
In some instances, sophisticated medical technologies have gotten a lot cheaper over time for citizen scientists to use—for instance, the cost of whole genome sequencing, which can reveal genetic risks for diseases, has dropped from around $1 million 20 years ago to around $500 today. But the price of turning insights and unconventional treatment suggestions from AI into medicines designed for a single person is still extremely expensive.
Alex Kalogeropoulos, for one, estimated the costs for the program he is pursuing for his wife at over $250,000 and counting. He compared the situation to when the first Teslas came on the market. “They made a very expensive roadster. Eventually they were able to make more mass-market car models,” he said.
In March, Kalogeropoulos asked two of the major chatbots—OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Anthropic’s Claude—to review Alicia’s brain imaging scans, medical records, blood tests and other data. He told them he and his wife didn’t want to pursue chemotherapy and radiation, even though Alicia’s doctors suggested they do so.
He asked the chatbots to propose multiple ways to attack the tumor while preserving her immune system. He encouraged them to make suggestions based on early research and to include vaccines and immunotherapies that might work on Alicia’s type of cancer even if they were being developed for other diseases. He also told the chatbots to present their plans in ways that her doctors would take seriously.
Next, he brought in Google’s Gemini and xAi’s Grok both to check the work done by ChatGPT and Claude and to make their own suggestions. He prompted Grok to bring the perspective of a “cutting edge oncologist” who recognized that “ultimately standard of care for her condition will fail and she will die.”
Alicia said she and Alex talked many times about the risks of forgoing chemo and radiation to give the AI plan time to work. “I’m confident in it,” she said. “We’re going to be talking to computers to try to figure things out because they’re a lot smarter than we are.”
Anyone can ask a chatbot for advice on how to treat a rare and aggressive cancer. If AI recommends bespoke medicines as part of their treatment plans, someone still needs to actually make them.
Through his own experience with cancer, Sijbrandij saw a void in the market for people who need that kind of help. The Sijbrandij Foundation started the Future of Cancer Care initiative, which helps patients for no charge to navigate options for personalized cancer treatment. His venture firm has now funded 10 startups dedicated to personalized medicine. (Sijbrandij won’t say how much money he put into the fund, which now has other limited partners in addition to him.)
One of the startups is Echo Immune, which helps people navigate the regulatory and scientific steps needed to make and administer personalized cancer vaccines. Another, Kernis Health, helps patients get samples of their tumors so they can later be analyzed or used to develop personalized vaccines.
Sijbrandij came up with the idea for Kernis with Bernardo Cordovez, the company’s CEO. Cordovez said that patients are frightened when they get a cancer diagnosis and often too overwhelmed to plan beyond getting the tumor out of the body.
By the time they realize they need access to tumor samples to pursue personalized treatments, it can be difficult to navigate a hospital’s bureaucracy and legal rules to get them back. Kernis ensures that patients can send some tumor tissue to other institutions or companies for storage.
Even as more companies emerge to provide services to wealthy citizen scientists, there are AI startups building new models that go beyond existing biological knowledge. One of them is Noetik, which has raised $56 million since 2023 and has run extensive tests on human tumor samples to train its AI models, according to Ron Alfa, founder and CEO of the San Francisco-based startup.
“The current AI models can reason across the things we already understand,” said Alfa. “But we are trying to take it to that next level.”
Noetik is building AI models to predict which cancer patients will benefit from which drug and is partnering with big pharma companies like GSK, which signed a $50 million deal earlier this year to license Noetik AI models. But last month, Alfa ran into Sijbrandij at an AI conference in San Francisco where they were both speakers. Alfa told him he wanted to help citizen scientists.
Sijbrandij connected him with Cordovez, who came to visit Noetik’s lab. Alfa and Cordovez are now trying to figure out a way for Cordovez’s customers to send tumor samples to Alfa’s startup so it can run tests for its AI models on them. In exchange, the startup would give the patients data back to use as they pursue their own treatments.
Alfa’s romantic partner, Elizabeth Brereton, died last year at the age of 44 after eight years battling sarcoma, a rare cancer. Alfa, who is a physician, said he had pushed Brereton’s doctors to think beyond the standard of care for her rare cancer and also try other medicines approved for different diseases. He believed these efforts had extended Brereton’s life longer than expected but hadn’t been enough to save her.
Ron Alfa and Elizabeth Brereton in Paris in 2022. Courtesy of Alfa
“The AI models were too late to help her,” he said.
Alfa said making a personalized cancer vaccine may help an individual but is too expensive to scale. AI models built on data from thousands of patients can lead to a better understanding of cancer biology and, hopefully, better drugs for more people.
“At the end of the day, we don’t want to cure one person’s cancer, we want to cure all cancers,” he said.
In December 2018, Alicia Kalogeropoulos was resting at home on the couch after a 24-hour shift as a nurse anesthetist when she got up to check on the dog. She slipped on the dog bed and landed hard on the floor. She went to the emergency room.
Imaging tests to rule out a concussion ended up revealing she had a brain tumor. She had surgery to remove it. Eighteen months later, a scan showed the tumor appeared to be back. She started the clinical trial for the pill that seemed to keep the cancer at bay until this February, when a scan revealed that the tumor “had exploded in size,” Alex Kalogeropoulos said.
Doctors told the couple that Alicia should do surgery again to try to remove the tumor. Once Alicia recovered, they would need to decide what to do next. The doctors suggested chemotherapy and radiation to try to buy her more time.
Alex wanted to consider more options and hit the tumor with more than one drug. He had seen a post on X from someone who had used Cordovez’s startup Kernis Health to help a loved one with a brain tumor. He arranged a phone call with Cordovez in late February to discuss doing more advanced testing on Alicia’s tumor beyond what is typically offered in the hospital’s labs.
Cordovez connected Alex with Jason Barbour, CEO of Echo Immune, the startup that helps shepherd clients through the process of making personal cancer vaccines. Alex talked with Barbour for over an hour trying to understand the process, which would likely take seven to eight weeks before Alicia’s vaccine was ready. The Kalogeropouloses decided to go ahead.
Alex  set up two computer displays on his desk and had the various chatbots running all hours of the day constructing an alternative treatment plan for Alicia. Each time he learned something new, he asked the chatbots for feedback.
Alex started calling the protocol that the chatbots came up with the Galileo Plan to reflect his desire to challenge the standard of care. It consisted of giving Alicia vaccines and other therapies, waiting a week or two before adding in something new to make sure there were no immediate problems. Among the recommendations are an FDA-approved anti-parasite medicine that had shown promise in fighting cancer in early animal studies.
When the couple first presented the AI plan to Alicia’s doctor, the surgeon said it appeared like a reasonable plan. Still, he also repeated his own suggestion. “I would be remiss not to suggest you do radiation at this time,” he told her. “Just in case.”
The couple took the warning seriously and had many talks about what to do. In the end, Alex said he felt reassured by the idea that if the Galileo Plan didn’t work, they could still do radiation. Alicia agreed.
Alicia Kalogeropoulos (left) with her husband Alex and their daughter Kora in Phoenix in March. Courtesy of Alex Kalogeropoulos
Cordovez flew to Phoenix, where Alicia was having surgery on March 17 to remove her tumor. He got the legal documents signed so part of the tumor could be flash frozen and sent to companies recommended by Cordovez to do the analysis to make the personalized vaccine.
In the meantime, the AI chatbots also recommended another kind of cancer vaccine, one that was immediately available and would tell Alicia’s immune system to attack a known mutation in her tumor. One of Alicia’s oncologists told Alex he could save money and get the vaccine more quickly if he bought it in Mexico.
Earlier this month, a driver took Alex from San Diego across the border to Mexico, dropping him at a medical clinic in Tijuana. He bought nine doses of the vaccine, put them in a cooler and returned to the U.S. This week, Alicia got her second dose.
“We’re going for a cure here,” she said. “And you can’t do that with the standard of care that they’ve been using for the last 50 years that isn’t saving people.”

标题: Musk-OpenAI and Big Tech Earnings on Deck This Week
内容:
This is going to be a fun week. First, Elon Musk and Sam Altman square off in a California court over the origins of OpenAI (for more details,
see here
). The final outcome probably won’t have a huge impact on the ChatGPT company—Musk’s chances of both winning the case and getting everything he wants from a victory seem unlikely,
as we wrote here
. No matter what happens, though, the courtroom antics should be entertaining. (Musk still has
a chance of winning
, to be clear).
And then on Wednesday, four of the biggest tech firms—Google, Meta Platforms, Microsoft and Amazon—are releasing their March quarter numbers after the market closes, likely within a few minutes of each other. (Coincidence, or conspiracy to bamboozle hapless reporters and analysts?) Apple follows on Thursday with its March quarter numbers.
Analysts expect Apple will deliver 15% revenue growth—similar to the previous quarter and a huge improvement on the single-digit growth (or declining) numbers we’ve been used to seeing from Apple in recent years. That will give retiring CEO Tim Cook one more chance to throw the word “record” around.
But it's the Wednesday reports that are most important. The four companies reporting are spending a combined $600 billion-plus on capex this year, much of that to expand AI-related data center capacity, so the simultaneous reports will offer a valuable report card on the state of play.
That’s more true of the three big cloud firms—Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud—which are renting out their servers for use by one or both of the major AI firms, OpenAI and Anthropic, along with a growing number of other businesses. In the fourth quarter, Google Cloud and Amazon Web both reported accelerating growth while Microsoft Azure
reported a slight slowdown
on a still-hot growth rate of 39%, due to a lack of computing capacity. That ensures all eyes will be on Azure’s growth rate this week.
But it’s not just about cloud growth. Investors have zeroed in on how Microsoft’s software business will fare in an AI-dominated world, putting the spotlight on the so-far relatively small number of subscriptions to its Copilot AI chatbot. Microsoft
has revamped its Copilot teams
so an update on Copilot subscriptions is highly anticipated.
Then there’s the advertising market, dominated by Google and Meta. Both tech giants have deployed AI to boost their ad businesses, although Meta seems to be outdoing its bigger rival. Analysts seem to be anticipating more of the same: Meta is expected to post its strongest growth rate since late 2021. Alphabet’s growth rate is also expected to be higher than in recent quarters, although to a less notable degree.
The bottom line is that a blizzard of numbers will arrive on Wednesday afternoon. Digging through the deluge will take a bit of time but it will be worth the effort.

Nvidia shares closed at an all time high of $208.27 on Friday, giving the company a market capitalization of $5.06 trillion.
•
Google has agreed to make an additional investment in Anthropic of up to $40 billion, deepening a multipronged relationship that also includes a major expansion of Anthropic’s access to AI servers. The search engine company, an early backer of Anthropic, will initially invest $10 billion in at a $350 billion valuation.

Schwarz Group, the German investment firm that is one of Aleph Alpha’s biggest backers, will also invest $600 million in Cohere’s upcoming Series E funding round, the companies said.
